title: VPN for Small Business 2026: What You Need, What to Skip, and the Best Options
description: A practical guide to VPNs for small businesses in 2026: when you actually need one, what features matter, and the best options by team size and use case.
content:
Small businesses are targeted by cybercriminals precisely because they have valuable data but lack enterprise-grade security. A VPN is one of the simplest protections you can add -- but only if you deploy it for the right reasons.

When a VPN Actually Protects Your Business

A VPN encrypts traffic between your employees' devices and the internet (or your company systems). This matters most in three situations. First: remote work over untrusted networks. An employee working from a coffee shop, airport, or hotel is on a network that others can monitor. A VPN encrypts that traffic so ISPs and other network users cannot intercept it. Second: accessing internal company resources. If you have a server, NAS, or internal web tool that should only be accessible to employees, a VPN creates a private tunnel. Employees connect to the VPN, then access internal resources as if they were on the office network. Third: regulatory or contractual confidentiality requirements. Law firms, accountants, healthcare practices, and financial advisors often have confidentiality obligations that extend to data in transit. A VPN is one of the technical controls that satisfies those requirements.

When a VPN Is Not What You Need

A VPN is not a substitute for other security basics. It does not protect against phishing (employees clicking bad links), weak passwords, unpatched software, or malware on employee devices. If your main concern is data breach from those vectors, a VPN adds little protection. A VPN also does not protect you from your own cloud services' security practices. If your business runs entirely on Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, and Salesforce -- all accessed via HTTPS -- your data in transit is already encrypted. Adding a VPN to that setup provides marginal additional security.

Consumer vs. Business VPN Plans

Most small businesses under 10 employees are well-served by consumer VPN plans. The key difference in business plans: a centralized admin dashboard where you can add and remove users, view connection logs (for compliance purposes), and enforce VPN usage policies. For a 3-person team where everyone manages their own device, a consumer plan at $4-8/month is sufficient. For a 15-person team where you need to provision and deprovision access as employees join and leave, the business management dashboard is worth the premium.

The Best Options in 2026

NordVPN Teams is the most popular small business VPN: clean admin interface, competitive $7/user/month pricing, and a strong no-logs track record backed by independent audits. ExpressVPN for Business offers a similar product with slightly better performance on international connections -- useful for businesses with employees in multiple countries. ProtonVPN Business is the best choice for businesses in regulated industries where jurisdiction matters. Proton is incorporated in Switzerland and is outside US and EU surveillance law jurisdiction. For technical founders comfortable with self-hosting: a WireGuard server on a $5/month VPS (DigitalOcean, Hetzner, Vultr) costs almost nothing per month and is easier to configure than older VPN protocols. The setup takes about 30 minutes with a tool like PiVPN or wg-easy.

Rollout Recommendations for Small Teams

Start with the devices that touch the most sensitive data: the laptop of your finance person, your developer workstation, any device used to access client files. Configure the VPN to auto-connect on untrusted networks. Do not require VPN for internal tools unless you have an actual internal server -- requiring VPN for tools that are already HTTPS-protected adds friction without security benefit. Establish a clear offboarding process: when an employee leaves, revoke their VPN credentials on the same day. This is the most commonly missed step in small business security offboarding.
